desc: Marrakech is a sensory-rich imperial city where medina souks, palace courtyards, and Atlas-framed light create a distinctive atmosphere. Visitors come for riad stays, craft traditions, historic monuments, and easy escapes to desert or mountain landscapes.
hls: Jemaa el-Fna square energy day and night
Dense souk network with traditional crafts
Major landmarks like Bahia Palace and Koutoubia
Gateway to Atlas Mountains and Agafay desert

todo.name: Jemaa el-Fna and souk circuit
todo.desc: Navigate the medina’s core lanes for spices, leatherwork, lamps, and nonstop street theater.

todo.name: Bahia Palace and Saadian Tombs
todo.desc: Tour two major sites showcasing Andalusian-Moroccan craftsmanship and dynastic heritage.

todo.name: Koutoubia and sunset terrace tea
todo.desc: See Marrakech’s defining minaret, then watch evening light from a nearby rooftop café.

todo.name: Majorelle Garden and YSL Museum
todo.desc: Pair cobalt botanical gardens with modern exhibits on fashion and Moroccan influence.

todo.name: Traditional hammam treatment
todo.desc: Experience scrub and steam rituals in either local bathhouses or upscale spa versions.

todo.name: Day trip to Atlas valleys or Agafay
todo.desc: Leave the city for Berber villages, mountain viewpoints, or stargazing desert camps.

wifi.note: Good in riads and modern hotels; medina properties can have weaker in-room signals due to old walls.

prac.fltNote: Gatwick–Marrakech has regular nonstop service year-round, often with strong shoulder-season fares.
prac.lang: Moroccan Arabic and Tamazight (French widely used)
prac.tz: GMT (UTC+0), GMT+1 during most of the year
prac.visa: Many passports (including US/UK/EU) can enter Morocco visa-free for up to 90 days; onward ticket evidence is recommended.
alerts: Friday prayer times and Ramadan hours can affect opening times for some shops and eateries.

sweet: March to May and October to November are best for city exploration; summer is intensely hot.
